← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Africa

Bronze, stone, cloth, and mud — craft from across the continent.

Africa's art tradition is vast and old. These pieces range from 9th-century Igbo bronzes to the largest mud-brick building in the world.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Blombos Cave Engravings
Middle Stone Age · c. 75,000 BCE · South Africa
Deliberate crosshatch patterns incised on ochre and shell beads perforated with bone awls — among the earliest evidence of symbolic thought ever found. The cave pushed back the dawn of human art by tens of thousands of years.
Apollo 11 Cave Stones
Namibia · c. 25,000 BCE
Seven palm-sized slabs painted in charcoal and ochre with therianthropic creatures — part-human, part-animal. Among the oldest figurative art anywhere in Africa.
Tsodilo Hills Rock Paintings
San · c. 24,000 BCE–1800 CE · Botswana
More than four thousand paintings on the quartzite outcrops the San call 'the Louvre of the desert.' Eland and giraffes predominate, but red-pigment handprints dating to the last Ice Age make this one of the longest continuously used sacred sites on earth.
Tassili n'Ajjer Rock Art
Saharan prehistoric · 10th millennium BCE onward · Algeria
Thousands of paintings and engravings high on a sandstone plateau — hunters, giraffes, cattle herds, swimmers. The Sahara when it was green.
Laas Geel Rock Paintings
Neolithic pastoralist · c. 3500 BCE · Somaliland
A complex of granite shelters filled with some of Africa's best-preserved rock art: cattle draped in ceremonial robes, their horns painted in alternating red and white, with small human figures raising arms beneath them. Only rediscovered by archaeologists in 2002.
Ring with a Scarab Bezel
by Egyptian
Egypt · middle kingdom · -1985–-1550 · Gold and green jasper
Funerary Papyrus of Tayuhenutmut
by Egyptian; probably Thebes
Egypt · third intermediate period · -1069–-945 · Papyrus and pigment

1000 BCE – 0

Yeha Great Temple
D'mt kingdom · c. 700 BCE · Ethiopia
A rectangular limestone sanctuary built without mortar, oriented to the rising of the moon god Almaqah. Its precise dry-stone masonry is the oldest standing building in Ethiopia and predates the Aksumite kings by nearly a millennium.
Nok Terracotta
Nok culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Nigeria
Hollow terracotta heads with triangular eyes and pierced pupils. The oldest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa.
Tetradrachm: Grapevine within Square and Incuse Square (reverse)
Greek, minted at Mende (Chalkidiki) · c. 430 BCE · silver
Tetradrachm: Dionysos on Donkey (obverse); Grapevine (reverse)
Greek, minted at Mende (Chalkidiki) · c. 430 BCE · silver
Tetradrachm: Dionysos with Kantharos on Donkey (obverse)
Greek, minted at Mende (Chalkidiki) · c. 430 BCE · silver

0 – 500 CE

San Rock Art
San · up to 30,000 years old · Southern Africa
Eland, hunters, and trance figures painted on rock shelters from Namibia to the Drakensberg. One of humanity's longest unbroken painting traditions.
Brandberg Rock Paintings
San · up to 2,000 years old · Namibia
The Brandberg Massif hides thousands of rock paintings, most famously the "White Lady" — not a lady at all, but a medicine figure. Painted for ritual, not ornament.
Twyfelfontein Petroglyphs
Pre-San hunter-gatherers · 6,000 years old · Namibia
Thousands of rock engravings of giraffes, rhinos, and lions on sandstone. A seal, 900 km from the nearest sea, suggests long-distance travel — or long memory.
Hairdressing as a Work of Art
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
Preparing for Marriage
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
A Blacksmith at Work'
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
A Strange Passport
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
Women Clearing Farm of Weeds
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
Making Water and Cooking Pots
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
A Native Orchestra
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
The Wonderful Tom-Tum of Umu-Nze
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
Making Palm-Leaf Mats for Thatching Purposes
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
The "Maw" (Spirit) of a Girl
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
Native Market. A Busy Scene
by Basden, George Thomas (OBE FRGS)
Nigeria · ca. early 20th century
Mask
by Ibibio
Nigeria · ibibio · Wood
Animal in the Form of a Pangolin
by Lega
Democratic Republic of the Congo · lega · Bone
Tombstone
Egypt · 6th century to 8th century
Sticharion
Egypt · 19th century
Vine Scrolls
Egypt · ca. 4th century to 5th century
Cupid
Egypt · 3rd century to 4th century
Naked male figure
Egypt · 3rd century to 4th century
Silenus
Egypt · 3rd century to 4th century
Aër
Egypt · 18th century
Nereid
Egypt · 3rd century to 4th century
Draped female figure
Egypt · 3rd century to 4th century
Fragment of a Tunic
by Egypt
Egypt · coptic (historically identified as) · 1–500 · Linen and wool
Fragment
by Egypt
Egypt · coptic (historically identified as) · 301–500 · Linen and wool
String of Beads
by Roman or Byzantine; Egypt
Egypt · roman period (egyptian) · 301–500 · Glass
Obelisks of Aksum
Aksumite kingdom · 4th c. · Ethiopia
Granite stelae marking royal graves; the tallest once stood nearly a hundred feet high. One was looted by Mussolini, returned in 2005.
Dancing Pan
Egypt, Coptic period · c. 350–500 · limestone with traces of red polychromy
Fragment of a Hanging
by Egypt
Egypt · coptic (historically identified as) · 401–600 · Linen and wool

500 – 1000

Lydenburg Heads
Early Iron Age · 500 CE · South Africa
Terracotta heads found buried under a farm in Mpumalanga — the earliest known African figurative sculpture south of the equator.
Hanging with Christian Images
Egypt, Byzantine period · 500s · dyed wool, undyed linen: plain weave (tabby) with inwoven tapestry weave
Curtain Panel with Scenes of Merrymaking
Egypt, Byzantine period · 500s · Linen, undyed; wool: dyed; tapestry weave with supplementary weft wrapping
Fragment from a Tunic
by Egypt
Egypt · coptic (historically identified as) · 501–700 · Linen and wool
Debre Damo Monastery
Aksumite · 6th century · Ethiopia
Perched atop a flat-topped mountain reachable only by a 15-meter rope hauled up by the monks. Its interior preserves carved wooden coffers and rafters that are the oldest surviving examples of Aksumite architecture.
Upper part of a gravestone.
Egypt · ca. 600-700
Roundel Fragment
by Egypt
Egypt · coptic (historically identified as) · 601–700 · Wool and linen
Rock-Hewn Churches of Tigray
Ethiopian Orthodox · 6th c. onward · Ethiopia
Dozens of churches carved directly into cliff faces and mountains, some older than Lalibela, many reached only by rope or handhold climbs. Still in active use.
Igbo-Ukwu Bronze
Igbo · 9th c. · Nigeria
Lost-wax castings of startling delicacy — three centuries before Ife, and predating most European bronzework of the same technical sophistication.

1000 – 1500

Mapungubwe Hill
Mapungubwe Kingdom · 1075–1220 · South Africa
A sandstone hill-top capital that controlled Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade centuries before Great Zimbabwe. A golden rhinoceros was dug up from a royal grave here.
Ethiopian Processional Cross
Ethiopian Orthodox · 12th c. onward · Ethiopia
Hand-held metal crosses carried at the front of church processions. Each is unique — geometries nested inside geometries, hammered out by a parish smith.
Cairo Citadel
Ayyubid → Ottoman · 1176 onward · Egypt
Saladin's fortress above old Cairo, topped centuries later by the Mosque of Muhammad Ali with its tall twin minarets — a silhouette that defines the skyline.
Great Mosque of Kilwa
Swahili coast · 11th–14th c. · Tanzania
Coral-stone mosque on an island that dominated Indian Ocean trade. Ibn Battuta called Kilwa "one of the most beautiful cities in the world."
Rock-Hewn Churches of Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · 12th–13th c. · Ethiopia
Eleven churches carved downward into solid rock. Meant as a new Jerusalem after pilgrimage routes were cut off.
Bandiagara Escarpment
Tellem and Dogon · c. 1000 CE onward · Mali
A 150-kilometer sandstone cliff that the Tellem used as burial shafts before the Dogon arrived in the fifteenth century. Granaries and shrines cling to the rockface like swallows' nests; the Dogon say their cosmology is written into the architecture.
Church of Saint George, Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · late 12th–early 13th century · Ethiopia
Carved top-down into a single block of red volcanic tuff in the shape of a Greek cross, thirty meters on a side. King Lalibela commissioned eleven such monolithic churches after Jerusalem fell to Saladin, intending to build a new Holy Land in the Ethiopian highlands.
Great Zimbabwe
Shona · 11th–15th c. · Zimbabwe
Dry-stone walls built without mortar, enclosing a trade capital that controlled the gold road to the Indian Ocean.
Great Mosque of Djenné
Mali · rebuilt 1907 (earlier mosque 13th c.)
The world's largest mud-brick building. Every spring the whole town replasters it by hand in a single day.
Golden Rhinoceros of Mapungubwe
Mapungubwe · c. 1220–1290 CE · South Africa
A small wooden figurine plated with hammered gold foil, unearthed from a royal grave on Mapungubwe Hill in 1934. Apartheid-era archaeology hid the find for decades because it proved sophisticated African statehood before European contact.
Bronze Head from Ife
Yoruba · 12th–15th c. · Nigeria
Naturalistic portraits cast in brass with lost-wax technique. When first seen in Europe they were disbelieved as African work.
Sankore Madrasa
Mali/Songhai · c. 989 onward · Timbuktu
An earthen mosque-university whose pyramidal minaret's dimensions match the Kaaba in Mecca. Its scholars curated one of the largest libraries in medieval Africa; tens of thousands of manuscripts from Sankore survive, hidden in family chests when Tuareg raiders closed in.
Ethiopian Mäsqälä Qǝddase
Ethiopian Orthodox · 13th–14th c.
A bronze hand cross with elaborate interlace — "cross of the Mass," carried by priests during the Qǝddase liturgy. Each Ethiopian cross design is an improvised variation on the theme.
Djinguereber Mosque
Mali Empire · 1327 · Timbuktu
Commissioned by Mansa Musa after his pilgrimage to Mecca. An Andalusian architect built it in earth and fossil limestone — one of three medieval mosques still standing in Timbuktu.
Dogon Cliff Villages
Dogon · 14th c. onward · Mali
Mud granaries and shrines stacked along the Bandiagara Escarpment. The Dogon moved here fleeing forced conversion and stayed half a millennium.
Timbuktu Manuscripts
West African Islamic · 13th c. onward · Mali
Hundreds of thousands of hand-copied manuscripts on astronomy, law, medicine, poetry. Families smuggled them out of Timbuktu in 2013 as jihadists approached, hiding them in cellars and metal chests.
Gedi Ruins
Swahili · 12th–17th c. · Kenya
A coral-stone town abandoned in the 17th century for reasons still debated — piracy, drought, Galla raids. Mosque, palace, houses with Chinese pottery fragments still in the walls.
Sapi Nomoli Figure
Sapi · Sierra Leone · 15th c. · soapstone
A soapstone seated figure carved by the Sapi before Portuguese contact — later farmers pulled these up from their fields, called them nomoli, and placed them in rice shrines as house-spirits.
Djenné-Djenno Terracotta Figure
Djenné-Djenno · Mali · 14th–17th c. · terracotta
A terracotta figure from sub-Saharan Africa's oldest city, found buried in abandoned house-floors. Most were looted in the 1970s–80s; provenance is now a permanent scandal of the trade.
Larabanga Mosque
Sudano-Sahelian · 15th c. · Ghana
A mud-and-stick mosque painted white, bristling with wooden supports. The oldest mosque in West Africa still in its original form.
Ethiopian Illuminated Bible
Ethiopian Orthodox · 15th c. onward
Ge'ez-language bibles painted on parchment with bold figural interior pages — a distinctly Ethiopian Christian visual tradition that ran continuously from late antiquity to the 20th century.
Cross
by Central Ethiopia
Ethiopia · ethiopia · 1450–1499 · Copper
Ethiopian Gospel Leaf
Ethiopian Orthodox · c. 1440–80
A single illuminated leaf from a gospel book, Saint Luke portrait painted in tempera on vellum. Ethiopian manuscripts kept this painterly tradition alive continuously from late antiquity through the 20th century.
Elmina Castle
Portuguese colonial · 1482 · Ghana
Whitewashed on a rock above the Gulf of Guinea — the oldest European building in sub-Saharan Africa. Started as a trading post for gold, became a holding point for the Atlantic slave trade.
Tomb of Askia
Songhai Empire · 1495 · Gao, Mali
A seventeen-meter pyramidal mud tower bristling with protruding palm-wood timbers, built for emperor Askia Mohammed after his pilgrimage to Mecca. The timbers double as permanent scaffolding for the annual replastering of its earthen skin.

1500 – 1800

Great Mosque of Kano
Hausa · 15th c. onward · Nigeria
A mud-and-stick Friday mosque rebuilt many times across six centuries. The current building rose after a 1937 fire; minarets slender as reeds above a walled city.
Swahili Stone Towns
Swahili coast · 12th–19th c. · East Africa
Coral-stone trading towns — Lamu, Pate, Kilwa — with carved wooden doors studded in brass, Indian Ocean dhows in their harbors, and mosques whose minarets face inland.
Khami Ruins
Torwa state · c. 1450–1650 · Zimbabwe
A stone city of terraced platforms retained by decorative chevron and checker patterns in dry-laid granite. Successor to Great Zimbabwe, Khami traded Ming porcelain and Portuguese beads with the east African coast before being burned in the seventeenth century.
Pendant Mask of Iyoba Idia
Edo / Benin Kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Ivory hip pendant worn at the Oba's waist — the queen mother's face framed by tiny carved Portuguese heads. Looted in 1897; a version is the emblem of FESTAC '77, Africa's mass Pan-African arts festival.
Gwandusu
Bamana · 15th–17th c. · Mali
A towering wooden mother-and-child figure — "extraordinary" is what the name means. Carved by a Bamana blacksmith for a men's association; the tallest, oldest intact Bamana figure known.
Equestrian Figure
by Dogon or Tellem
Mali · dogon · 1500–1900 · Wood and sacrificial material
Plaque
by Edo, Kingdom of Benin
Nigeria · edo (african) · 1501–1700 · Brass
Bronze Head of Queen Idia
Benin kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Portrait of the first Queen Mother of Benin, cast to sit on an ancestral altar. One of the most famous of the Benin bronzes.
Queen Mother Pendant Mask (Iyoba)
Edo kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Ivory mask worn on the belt of the Oba of Benin. The forehead tiaras are miniature Portuguese heads — trading partners carved as ornament.
Harar Jugol
Walled old city · 16th century · Ethiopia
Called Islam's fourth holy city, with 82 mosques and 102 shrines packed inside its five-gated wall. Harari houses are painted bright interior hues and display hung baskets — the craft tradition of married women signaling wealth and taste.
Benin Commemorative Head (Uhunmwun-Elao)
Benin Kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
A brass head of a dead oba, intended for his ancestral altar — one of the earliest surviving Benin heads, smaller and less stylized than the later ones before looting.
Benin Bronzes
Edo kingdom · 16th–17th c. · Nigeria
Plaques that once covered pillars of the royal palace in Benin City. Looted in 1897; still being repatriated today.
Tapper (Iroke Ifa)
by Yoruba
Nigeria · yoruba · 1601–1800 · Ivory
Fasil Ghebbi
Ethiopian · 17th c. · Gondar
A fortress-city of stone castles built by a succession of Solomonic emperors. The European-looking keeps are misleading — they're Ethiopian invention, not import.
Kente Cloth
Asante & Ewe · 17th c. onward · Ghana
Hand-woven silk and cotton strips sewn together into royal cloth. Each pattern has a name and a proverb behind it.
Akan Goldweights
Asante / Akan · 15th–19th c. · Ghana
Brass figurines used to weigh gold dust. Each one is also a proverb — the weight you balance against is a riddle.
Ndop — Kuba King Portrait
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Wooden royal portraits showing each Kuba king with a personal emblem — a drum, an anvil, a game board. Stand-ins who keep the king present after death.
Baule Sculpture
Baule · 17th c. onward · Côte d'Ivoire
Delicate wooden figures of "spirit spouses" from a parallel world, and calm portrait masks carved for Goli festivals. Among the most influential African sculptural traditions.
Head
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Akan-style artist · 1650–1700 · terracotta
Commemorative Head
by Edo, Court of Benin
Nigeria · edo (african) · 1650–1750 · Terracotta
Spoon
Ethiopia · 1650-1710
Cape Coast Castle
Colonial fortification · 1653 onward · Ghana
A whitewashed castle on the Gulf of Guinea used for the Atlantic slave trade — enslaved Africans passed through its "Door of No Return." Now a museum, with whitewash still yearly.
Triptych Icon
by Central Ethiopia
Ethiopia · gondar · 1682–1706 · Tempera on linen
Koutammakou (Takienta Tower-Houses)
Batammariba · 17th century onward · Togo/Benin
Fortified two-story mud towers with conical thatched granary turrets, built by the Batammariba on the Togo-Benin border. Each house is read as a human body — entrance mouth, granary head — and its construction ritualized every stage of a family's life.
Akan Memorial Head (Nsodie)
Akan · late 17th c. · Ghana
A terracotta portrait head made at the death of a royal — abstracted features, shaved pattern on the crown. Akan nsodie were modeled by women potters, stood upright on graves, and left to weather back into earth.
Head (Uhunmwun Elao)
by Edo, Kingdom of Benin
Nigeria · edo (african) · 1701–1825 · Brass
Golden Stool of the Asante
Asante · 18th c. · Ghana
Sacred seat that is said to contain the soul of the Asante nation. No one — not even the king — sits on it. A British demand to do so in 1900 sparked war.
Adinkra Symbols
Akan / Asante · 18th c. onward · Ghana
A system of visual proverbs stamped or woven into cloth — each glyph carrying a name and an aphorism. Worn at funerals; later embraced globally as a Pan-African vocabulary.
Yoruba Altar Figure
Yoruba · 18th c. · Nigeria
A copper-alloy onílé figure that stood on a shrine — "owner of the house" — tall, delicate, hands held up in salutation. Yoruba brass casting far from Benin's more famous tradition.
Yäʿğğ Mäsqäl
Ethiopian · 18th c. · Tigray
A wood-and-metal hand cross used for personal blessing — the priest kisses it, presses it to the forehead of each worshipper. Lattices of ornamental crosses ring the central form.
Kesa
by Japan
Japan · edo (japanese period) · 1775–1800

1800 onward

Kuba Appliqué Textiles
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Raffia cloth embroidered with cut-pile geometrics. Men weave the base; women embroider. No two patterns repeat — each is a one-time composition.
Senufo Nursing Woman
Senufo · 18th–20th c. · Côte d'Ivoire
A wooden mother-and-child figure, baby at her breast, body elongated and severe. Used in the Poro men's initiation society — the woman the boys are being taught to leave behind.
Male Figure
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kongo or Vili, early 19th century · early 1800s · Wood, iron, and metalized glass
Staff of Office
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Luba · early to mid-1800s · Wood and iron
Bow Stand
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style carver · 1800s · Wood and plant fiber
Disk Pendant (akrafokɔnmu)
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Asante Empire/Kingdom, probably Kumasi, member of the goldsmiths’ guild · 1800s, before 1896 · Gold
Gold Weight (abrammuo): Geometric Form
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Akan-style artist · 1800s · Copper alloy
Gold Weight (abrammuo): Antelope
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Akan-style artist · 1800s · Copper alloy
Knife-sheath tip (sika boha)
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Asante Empire/Kingdom, Kumasi or probably Kumasi, member of the goldsmiths' guild · 1800s · Gold
Idiophone/Staff (ahianmwẹn-ọrọ)
Nigeria, Benin Kingdom, Ẹdo peoples, members of the Igun Eronmwon (royal brasscasters) guild · possibly 1800s · Copper alloy
Neckpiece with panel (umphapheni)
Africa, Southern African, South Africa, Southeast Cape Region, Northern Nguni (Zulu)-style maker · 1800s–1900s · Glass beads, plant fiber, copper alloy, and iron
Prestige Staff
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Tsonga-style or Zulu-style maker · 1800s–1900s · Wood
Necklace (amazipho)
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Southeast Cape Region, Northern Nguni (Zulu)-style maker · 1800s · Glass beads, bone, and sinew
Pair of Ritual Staffs (ẹdan Ògbóni)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · possibly 1800s · Copper alloy and iron
Throwing Knife
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Oriental Province, Mangbetu, 19th century · 1800s · Iron and wood
Sickle knife (trumbash)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Mangbetu maker · 1800s · Iron and wood
Headrest
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu-style carver, Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Kingdom of Eswatini, Swazi-style carver · 1800s–1900s · Wood
Hand Coil
Nigeria, Benin Kingdom, Ẹdo peoples, members of the Igun Eronmwon (royal brasscasters) guild · 1800s · Copper alloy
Sculptural Element from a Reliquary Ensemble (mbulu ngulu)
Africa, Central Africa, Gabon, Kota-style maker · probably 1800s · Wood, copper alloy, and iron
Snuff Container
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Zimbabwe or Mozambique, Northern Nguni-style or Shona-style maker · 1800s–1900s · Gourd and copper alloy
Prestige stool (Kuo fo)
Africa, Central Africa, Cameroon, Bandjoun Kingdom, Bamileke makers · possibly 1800s · Wood, cotton, plant fiber, glass beads, and indigo
Belt (Yeemy Mambolmashet)
by Kuba
Democratic Republic of the Congo · kuba (democratic republic of congo style · 1801–1900 · Jute
Pendant Belt (Nkody Mupaap)
by Kuba
Democratic Republic of the Congo · kuba (democratic republic of congo style · 1801–1900 · Jute
Headrest (Isicamelo or Isigqiki)
by Zulu
South Africa · zulu · 1801–1900 · Wood
Four Bracelets
by Igbo
Nigeria · igbo · 1801–1899 · Ivory
Uchikake (Over Kimono)
by Japan
Japan · late edo · 1801–1900 · Silk
Female Figure (Bateba Phuwe)
by Lobi
Burkina Faso · lobi · 1801–1900 · Wood and sacrificial material
Ancestral Altar Tusk
Nigeria, Benin Kingdom, Ẹdo peoples, members of the Igbesanmwan (wood and ivory carvers) guild · c. 1820 · Elephant ivory
Esu Dance Staff (Ogo Elegbara)
by Attributed to an unidentified Ketu master
Nigeria · yoruba · 1825–1900 · Wood
Double Panel
by Possibly Mangbetu
Democratic Republic of the Congo · mangbetu · 1825–1900 · Raffia
Headdress (Jagún Jagún)
by Yòrùbá
Nigeria · yoruba · 1834–1900 · Wood
Flywhisk
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, Baule-style maker · c. 1850 · Copper alloy and leather
Senufo Sculpture
Senufo · West Africa
Elongated female figures and carved divination stools used by the Poro and Sandogo societies in Côte d'Ivoire and Mali.
Ibeji Twin Figures
Yoruba · 19th c. onward · Nigeria
When a twin dies, a carved figure stands in — fed, dressed, and cared for like the living. The Yoruba have one of the highest twinning rates in the world.
Asante Jewelry
Akan / Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
Gold jewelry for the chief and his court — beads, pectorals, rings cast with the same lost-wax technique as the goldweights. Sumptuary law regulated who could wear what.
Tuareg Metalwork
Tuareg · 18th c. onward · Sahara
Silver crosses, amulet cases, saddle fittings. Each Tuareg town has a signature cross shape; the Agadez cross is the most widely reproduced.
Ikenga
Igbo · pre-1900 · southeast Nigeria
Personal shrines carved as horned male figures, each one owned by a man and activated through sacrifice to embody the 'right hand' — the strength that accomplishes things. When the owner dies the Ikenga is split in two.
Èṣù Dance Staff
Yoruba · 19th c. · Nigeria
A staff topped with a kneeling figure wearing a phallic topknot — Èṣù, the trickster-messenger who stands at every crossroads. Cowries dangle from the shaft; the staff was swung in ritual dance.
Asante Gold Lion Ornament
Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
A small cast-gold lion worn by royal courtiers — the Asante king's gold-staff bearers, messengers, and swordbearers all carried miniatures that marked their role.
Asante Knife-Sheath Tip (Sika Boha)
Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
A cast-gold terminal for a ceremonial knife sheath, the gold pounded and modeled into tiny emblematic forms — hidden in the court's regalia and worn only on state occasions.
Chokwe Ceremonial Throne
Chokwe · 19th c. · Angola
A backed wooden chair (citwamo ca mangu) carved with figures standing, sitting, copulating along the arms and rungs — royal regalia adapted from European chairs seen via Portuguese trade.
Kuba Bwoom Mask
Kuba · Congo · mid–late 19th c. · wood and paint
An initiation helmet mask with bulging forehead — Bwoom represents a commoner or prince opposite two royal masks; at masquerades the three perform a mythic family argument.
Yombe Phemba Mother and Child
Yombe · Congo · mid–late 19th c. · wood
A seated Yombe mother with her child — phemba figures held fertility medicine in a cavity in the back, carried by women struggling to conceive.
Lega Bwami Iginga Figure
Lega · Congo · 19th c. · elephant ivory
A small ivory figurine used in the Bwami initiation — each iginga embodied a proverb the initiate had to memorize. Bwami is an ethical order: the higher the rank, the more elaborate the ivory.
Comb (cisakulo)
Africa, Central Africa, Angola, or Democratic Republic of Congo, Chokwe-style · mid- to late 1800s · Wood, glass beads, and natural fibers
Male Face Mask (Chihongo)
by Chokwe
Angola · chokwe · 1850–1900 · Wood
Royal Chair (Akonkromfi)
by Asante
Ghana · asante · 1850–1875 · Wood
Horn
by Kuba
Democratic Republic of the Congo · kuba (democratic republic of congo style · 1850–1899 · Wood
Pendant: Crocodile
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, probably Baule-style goldsmith · 1930 or earlier · Gold
Ifá divination vessel (àgéré Ifá)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, possibly Èkìtì State, Yorùbá-style carver · mid- late 1800s · Wood and colorant
Altar Stool
by Attributed to an unidentified Ketu master
Nigeria · yoruba · 1850–1900 · Wood and pigment
Headdress (Ago Egungun)
by Ojerinde
Nigeria · yoruba · 1850–1899 · Wood and pigment
Tusk
by Edo, Kingdom of Benin
Nigeria · edo (african) · 1850–1888 · Ivory
Beaded Necklace
Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu · by 1942 · Plant fibers, glass beads, possibly sinew, and copper alloy button
Ritual Head
by Hemba
Democratic Republic of the Congo · hemba · 1850–1900 · Wood
Processional cross
Ethiopia · before 1868
Sowei Helmet Mask
Mende · 19th c. onward · Sierra Leone
A black helmet mask worn in the Sande women's society initiation — the only African mask tradition danced exclusively by women, representing ideals of beauty and comportment.
Arm ring
South Africa · ca. 1870-1880
Leg ring
South Africa · 1870-1880
Goldweight
Ghana · before 1874
Pipe
Ghana · before 1874
Ornament
Ghana · before 1874
Badge
Ghana · before 1874
Head of a Hobbyhorse (korèdugaso)
Africa, West Africa, Mali, Bamana blacksmith · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood and iron
Helmet
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood and paint
Mask for Egungun (Ere Egungun)
by Yoruba
Nigeria · yoruba · 1875–1900 · Wood and pigment
Carved Tooth
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Cabinda, or Republic of the Congo, probably Vili-style maker · late 1800s · Hippopotamus ivory and organic material
Ceremonial Skirt
by Kuba, Bushong
Democratic Republic of the Congo · kuba kingdom · 1875–1900 · Raffia
Figural Staff
by Baule
Côte d'Ivoire · baule · 1875–1900 · Wood and iron
Snuff Spoon (intshengula or izintshengula)
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu-style maker · late 1800s · bone
Club (Knobkerrie)
Southern Africa, South Africa, Northern Ngun-style or Zulu-style maker · late 1800s or early 1900s · Wood
Snuff Spoon/Comb (intshengula or izintshengula)
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu-style maker · late 1800s · Bone
Twin Figure (Ère Ìbejì)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, copper alloy, iron alloy, lead, glass beads, cowrie shells, twine, and pigment
Mask (Kple Kple or Kouassi Gbe)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d’Ivoire, Baule-style carver · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood, resin, and paint
Ceremonial Ladle (Wunkirmian or Wakemia)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, Dan-style carver · possibly late 1800s or early 1900s · Wood, twine, and possibly resin
Male Figure (Ofika)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Mbole-style carver · late 1800s or early 1900s · Wood, colorant, and upholstery studs
Zulu Beadwork
Zulu · 19th c. onward · South Africa
Glass trade beads sewn onto leather and cotton in geometric patterns — colors and combinations encoding courtship messages ("love letters" in bead).
Chiwara Headdress
Bamana · 19th–20th century · Mali
A roan antelope carved in openwork wood worn by young men who danced in pairs at planting season. The Bamana say Chiwara is the mythical being who first taught humans to farm, bent over the earth like the antelope's graceful curve.
Kota Reliquary Figure
Kota · 19th c. · Gabon
A flat, copper-sheeted abstract face on a diamond-shaped body — the guardian of a basket of ancestral bones. The staring form entranced Picasso and the Cubists when it arrived in Paris.
Kongo Crucifix
Kongo · late 19th c. · Central Africa
A carved crucifix with Christ depicted with Kongo features and a flat, wide-nosed face — Christianity arrived with the Portuguese in 1491, and Kongo Christ became genuinely Kongo.
Kongo Prestige Cap (Mpu Ngola)
Kongo · 19th c. · DRC
A conical raffia cap crowned with glass beads and brass tacks, worn by chiefs and diviners. The "mpu" projected rank visible across the village square.
Kongo Power Figure (Nkisi)
Kongo · late 19th c. · Central Africa
A wooden figure accumulating nails, blades, and resin packets — each added by a ritual specialist to bind a specific prayer or curse. The object is a growing archive of contracts.
Corpus (Crucified Christ)
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Cabinda, Kongo people · late 1800s-early 1900s · Copper alloy
Male figure (nkisi nkondi)
Democratic Republic of Congo or Cabinda (Angola), Kongo artist and nganga (priest) · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood, metalized glass, iron, organic material, ivory or plastic, and natural fiber
Power figure (nkisi)
Africa, Central Africa, Republic of the Congo, Kongo-style maker · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood, organic materials (including resin), cloth, glass, and various other materials
Female Bowl-Bearing Figure (lid)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style carver · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood and upholstery studs
Figure (Nkishi)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, organic materials (including resin and soil), plant fiber, and glass beads
Female Bowl-Bearing Figure
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style carver · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood, glass beads, upholstery studs, plant fiber, and iron
Pendant
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Ivory
Divination Implement
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, glass beads, and natural fiber
Male Figure (nkishi)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Songye-style carver and nganga (priest) · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, glass beads, brass (including upholstery studs), copper alloy, iron alloy, raffia, reedbuck antelope horn, rawhide, animal hair, human teeth, organic material, minerals, and plant fibers
Female Figure
Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Songye people · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood, upholstery studs, antelope horns, and animal hair
Snuff Mortar (Tesa Ya Ma Kanya)
Africa, Central Africa, Angola or Democratic Republic of Congo, Chokwe carver · late 1800s · Wood, upholstery studs, cloth, and metal
Whistle
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Angola, Chokwe-style maker · late 1800s · Probably hippopotamus ivory
Lamellophone (chisanji)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Angola, Chokwe-style maker · late 1800s · Wood, iron, and wire
Female Figure (akua'ba)
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Fante-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, hair, resin, and bone
Oliphant (Side-Blown Trumpet)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo (most likely), Cabinda, or Republic of the Congo, probably Yombe-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Ivory
Male Figure from a Pair (asye usu)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d’Ivoire, Baule-style carver · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, resin, glass beads, plant fiber, and metal
Female Figure from a Pair (asye usu)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d’Ivoire, Baule-style carver · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, resin, glass beads, plant fiber, and metal
Figure Pair (asye usu)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d’Ivoire, Baule-style carver · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, resin, glass beads, plant fiber, and metal
Mother-and-Child Figure
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Pende-style maker · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, glass beads, upholstery studs, natural fiber, and pigment
Figure (kiteki)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Yaka-style carver · late 1800s–mid-1900s · Wood, lizard skin, leopard tooth, pods, cloth, and natural fiber
Mask (hemba)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Suku-style carver · late 1800s–early 1900s · Wood, reed, paint, and metal
Janus Figure
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Hemba-style maker · late 1800s-early 1900s · Wood
Kasubi Tombs
Buganda kingdom · 1882 · Uganda
The burial palace of four kabakas of Buganda, a vast thatched dome called Muzibu-Azaala-Mpanga supported by rings of wooden columns wrapped in bark cloth. Fire destroyed the main structure in 2010 and a painstaking reconstruction is ongoing.
Knife Handle Cover
Africa, West Africa, Ghana, Asante Empire/Kingdom, Kumasi or probably Kumasi, member of the goldsmiths' guild · 1800s · Gold
Baga Serpent Headdress
Baga · Guinea · late 19th–early 20th c. · wood and paint
A tall painted wooden serpent worn above the dancer's head. When colonial missions banned the Baga cult in the 1950s the masks burned or sold abroad; the form was revived in 1985.
Carving
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kongo, early 20th century · c. 1900 · ivory
Caryatid Stool
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style carver · c. 1900 · wood
Nkisi Power Figure
Kongo · 19th–20th c. · Central Africa
Wooden figures bristling with nails and blades. Each nail driven in activated a pledge, a curse, or a contract witnessed by the spirit inside.
Fang Reliquary (Byeri)
Fang · 19th–20th c. · Gabon / Cameroon
Carved guardian heads placed atop boxes of ancestral bones. Picasso saw one in 1907 and the Demoiselles d'Avignon followed.
Akuaba
Akan · traditional · Ghana
Wooden fertility figures carried by women hoping to conceive. Small, abstracted, round-faced. Carved also now for collectors; the sacred ones are never sold.
Herero Dress
Herero · late 19th c. onward · Namibia
Floor-length Victorian gowns adopted from 19th-century German missionaries — kept, inverted, made defiantly Herero. The horned headdress honors cattle; the silhouette accuses a colonial past.
Kanaga Mask
Dogon · 19th–20th c. · Mali
Tall crossbar mask worn at funerary dama dances. The double-plank form is read as sky-over-earth, God-above-man, or a bird in flight depending on the dancer.
Kuosi Elephant Mask
Bamileke · 19th–20th c. · Cameroon
A hooded bead-and-cloth mask of the Kuosi secret society. The long panels hanging front and back are the elephant's trunk; wearing it, a Bamileke elite danced a living symbol of royal power.
Chi Wara Antelope Headdress
Bamana · 19th–20th c. · Mali
Carved wooden antelopes worn on the head during planting rituals, male and female dancing as a pair. Chi Wara is the spirit who taught humans to farm.
Luba Lukasa
Luba · 19th c. onward · DRC
A wooden memory board studded with beads and pegs — a tactile mnemonic for court history, used by trained ritualists. No two lukasa are meant to be read exactly the same way.
Songye Nkishi Figure
Songye · 19th–20th c. · DRC
Bulky wooden figure bristling with nails, horns, brass tacks, beads — each addition a prayer, accusation, or cure. Kept by a community-appointed specialist, fed with offerings over generations.
Senufo Rhythm Pounder
Senufo · 19th–20th c. · Côte d'Ivoire
A tall, slender standing figure beaten against the ground to mark the rhythm of funerary processions — the Deble. Its abraded feet testify to decades of use.
Ifá Diviner's Necklace
Yoruba · 19th–20th c. · Nigeria
A beaded necklace worn by babaláwos — priests of Ifá divination. Colors encode the 256 figures of the Odu corpus, the Yoruba system for reading a client's fate.
Yoruba Beaded Crown
Yoruba · 19th–20th c. · Nigeria
A conical bead-veiled crown (adéńlá) worn only by a dynastic king — the veil hides the face because the oba's power is too much to meet directly. Made of tens of thousands of imported glass beads.
Lobi Baàthíl
Lobi · c. 1900 · Burkina Faso
A small stiff wooden ancestor figure (baàthíl) kept on a household shrine. Lobi figures guard against illness and misfortune — if they fail they are disciplined, buried, or burned.
Nesting Wedding Baskets
Western Sudan · c. 1900 · Burkina Faso region
A set of ten coiled baskets nesting into one another, each smaller than the last — part of a bride's dowry, filling a single large vessel that would be opened at the husband's family house.
Mangbetu Figurative Harp (Domu)
Mangbetu · Congo · early 20th c. · wood and rawhide
An arched harp topped with a female head in elongated Mangbetu style — head-binding was a real beauty practice, and musicians in the 1920s Ituri played these at King Okondo's palace.
Bamileke Elephant Mask (Mbap Mteng)
Bamileke · Cameroon Grassfields · early 20th c. · beads on cloth
A beaded cloth elephant mask with long trunk panels — worn by members of the Kuosi society, it declared the wearer's wealth through thousands of imported glass beads.
Mask (Omono or Tana Wurum Bogoro)
Africa, West Africa, Mali, Dogon-style blacksmith-carver · early 1900s · Wood and paint
Ring
Africa, West Africa, Mali, Dogon-style maker · before 1975 · Copper alloy
Helmet Mask
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, Senufo-style carver · early to mid-1900s · Wood, upholstery studs, and colorant
Bird Standard
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, Senufo-style carver · early to mid-1900s · Wood and upholstery studs
Hornbill
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, Senufo style, unknown maker · 1900s · Copper alloy
Headdress (chi wara)
Africa, West Africa, Mali, Bamana-style blacksmith-carver · early to mid-1900s · Wood, cowrie shells, glass beads, possibly aluminum, iron alloy, upholstery studs, and natural fibers
Double-Headed Headdress (N'gonzon Koun)
Africa, West Africa, Mali, Bamana-style blacksmith-carver · 1900s · Wood and iron
Staff
Africa, West Africa, Mali, Bamana-style blacksmith-carver · 1900s · Iron
Figure
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Luba, 20th century · 1900s · ivory
Comb
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style maker · c. 1900s? · Wood and wire
Scepter
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style maker · early 1900s? · Wood
Hair Ornament
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Luba-style maker · Unassigned, before 1915 · Iron
Sifting Basket
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Angola, possibly Chokwe-style maker · before 1915 · Plant fibers and dye
Wig (uyembe matota, wiyembe, or pulumba)
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Angola, Chokwe, early to mid 20th century · early to mid 1900s · Plant fiber, pelt, and clay
Headpiece (nlo-o-ngo)
Africa, Central Africa, Gabon, Fang style, unknown maker · early 1900s · Plant fibers, cloth, hair, cowrie shells, buttons, upholstery studs, and thread
Bracelet
Equatorial Africa, Gabon, Fang · 1900s · Copper alloy
Coronet (oríkògbófo)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · 1900s · Cloth, glass beads, and thread
Egúngún Masquerade Dance Costume
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · 1900s · cotton, velvet, flannel, leather, dye, probably wood, cowrie shells, metal
Back Skirt/Sitting Pad (negbe)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Mangbetu-style makers · by 1928 · Plant fibers and dye
Stool
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu-style maker · by 1928 · wood
Back Skirt / Sitting Pad (negbe)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu-style maker · before 1929 · Plant fibers and dye
Part of a chair
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Mangbetu style, unknown carver · 1900s · Wood
Vessel
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu, early 20th century · early 1900s · terracotta
Bowharp
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu-style maker · by 1928 · Wood, rawhide, plant fiber, metal, and glass bead
Basket
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu-style maker · by 1928 · Plant fibers and dye
Hat
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu-style maker · by 1928 · Plant fibers and dye
Royal Belt (yet)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Kuba Kingdom, Kuba-style maker · possibly early 1900s · Plant fiber, rawhide or leather, glass beads, seashells, cowrie shells, cloth, and colorant
Hat (Kalyeem)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · possibly early 1900s · Raffia, glass beads, cowrie shells, cloth (including wool), thread, and copper alloy
Cap
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Plant fibers, cloth, glass beads, cowrie shells, and copper alloy
Hat (laket) with Pin
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · Early 1900s · Raffia, dye, and metal
Hat (laket mishiing) with a pin
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Raffia, metal, and dye
Bowl
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Earthenware
Pin for a Hat
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Metal
Hat (laket)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Raffia and dye
Kuba cloth
Congo · 1900-1949
Mask (wan-balinga)
Africa, West Africa, Burkina Faso, Mossi-style blacksmith-carver · early 1900s · Wood and paint
Portrait Mask (mblo)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d’Ivoire, Baule-style carver · early to mid-1900s · Wood and paint
Pendant: Face
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, possibly Baule-style goldsmith · 1900s · Gold
Pendant: Face in Crescent
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, probably Baule-style goldsmith · 1900s · Gold
Pendant: Face in Diamond
Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, probably Baule-style goldsmith · 1900s · Gold
Pot
Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu-style potter · 1900s · Terracotta
Pot Lid
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu-style maker · 1900s · Plant fiber
Lidded Pot
Southern Africa, South Africa, Zulu, mid-20th century · 1900s · Terracotta and plant fiber
Diviner's Bag (Apo Ifa)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · 1900s · Leather, glass beads, and natural fiber
Divination Tray (ọpọ́n Ifá)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Yorùbá-style maker · 1900s · wood
Sheath
Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu, 20th century · 1900s · Leather and tin
Dagger
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mangbetu-style maker · 1900s · Iron and elephant ivory
Pin (ndwong angwoong) for Hat
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Metal
Pin (ndwong angwoong) for a Hat
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kuba-style maker · early 1900s · Metal
Hat (laket mishiing) with Pin
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, early 20th century · early 1900s · Raffia, metal, and dye
Heddle Pulley (Bo Nun Amuin mask)
Africa, West Africa, Côte d’Ivoire, Baule-style carver · 1900s · Wood, metal, and plant fiber
Ceremonial Hoe
Africa, Southern Africa, South Africa, possibly Zulu-style maker · early 1900s · Wood and copper alloy
Shrine Figure (Ikenga)
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Igbo carver · possibly early 1900s · Wood, organic materials, and raffia
Pair of Dolls
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Ibibio style, unknown artist · 1900s · Wood, kaolin, and paint
Doll
Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Ibibio style, unknown artist · 1900s · Wood, kaolin, and paint
Hen
Nigeria, Benin Kingdom, Ẹdo peoples, member(s) of the Igbesanmwan (wood and ivory carvers) guild · 1900s · wood
One of Ten Nesting Wedding Baskets
Africa, Western Africa, Burkina Faso, Lobi–style maker · c. 1900s? · Plant fiber and leather
Stopper for Ten Nesting Wedding Baskets
Africa, Western Sudan, Burkina Faso, Lobi, 20th century (?) · c 1900s ? · Plant fiber and leather
Set of Nesting Wedding Baskets
Africa, Western Sudan, Burkina Faso, Lobi, 20th century (?) · c. 1900s ? · Plant fiber and leather
Whistle (kasengosengo)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Pende-style maker · before 1915 · wood
Mask (ndeemba)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Yaka-style carver · early 1900s · Wood, raffia, paint, and cotton
Ceremonial Axe (gano)
Southern Africa, Zimbabwe, Shona-style blacksmith-carver · 1900s, by 1928 · Iron, wood, and metal
Headdress (Zigiren-Wɔndɛ)
Africa, West Africa, Guinea, Baga-style carver · early to mid-1900s · Wood, glass beads, upholstery studs, and natural fiber
Man's Bwami Prestige Cap (sawamazembe)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Lega-style maker · early to mid-1900s · Plant fiber, seashells, seeds, pods, buttons, and probably cotton
Bwami Prestige Cap (Mukuba or Nkumbu)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Lega-style maker · early to mid-1900s · Plant fibers, elephant rawhide and hair, cowrie shells, glass beads, and cord
Prestige Headdress (Ashetu or shüötu)
Bamum style, unknown maker, Grassfields region, Cameroon · 1900–1950 · cotton, dye (including indigo)
Royal Retainer
Equatorial Africa, Cameroon, Bamum, early 20th century · early 1900s · wood
Headdress
Africa, Central Africa, Cameroon, Grassfields region, Bamileke-style maker · 1900s · African gray parrot feathers, wood, plant fiber, probably cotton, string, and colorant
Female Mask (Gambanda or Kambanda)
Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Pende-style maker · early 1900s · Wood, plant fiber, and colorant
Shrine Figure for Sàngó
Yoruba · c. 1905 · Nigeria
A carved wooden shrine figure for the thunder-god Sàngó — kneeling, holding a double-axe staff, head crowned with a wide Sàngó coif. Among the few Yoruba sculptures attributed to a named workshop.
Headdress Mask: Buffalo
Africa, Central Africa, Cameroon, Grassfields region, Bamileke-style maker · c. 1910 · Wood and colorant
Dan Face Mask
Dan · early 20th c. · Liberia / Côte d'Ivoire
A small polished mask — serene, almost hypnotically calm. The Dan made finely finished masks to house gle spirits summoned for judgments, entertainments, or wrestling matches.
Agboho Mmuo Mask
Igbo · early 20th c. · Nigeria
A wooden "maiden spirit" mask, painted white with delicately slitted eyes and painted lips — the kind of face Igbo men wore to embody the spirits of beautiful young women.
Benin Rattle Staff (Ukhuhre)
Benin Kingdom · early 20th c.
A carved wooden staff shaken during altar rituals to summon ancestral spirits. Each court official had a rattle staff marking his office.
Yoruba Gelede Mask
Yoruba · early 20th c. · Nigeria
A carved wooden mask for a Gelede festival — the Yoruba annual rite honoring mothers and elderly women, whose "witchcraft" powers had to be appeased by men dancing as women.
Portrait Figure of Metang, the 10th King of Batufam
by Carved by Mbeudjang
Cameroon · bamileke · 1912–1914 · Wood and pigment with traces of chalk and camwood
Hemba Ancestor Figure
Hemba · 19th–20th c. · DRC
A standing male figure, hands on belly, face serene — a portrait of a clan ancestor rather than a generic spirit. The Hemba treat these statues as functional genealogies in wood.
Mbuti Bark Cloth
Mbuti · traditional · Ituri Rainforest
Beaten fig-bark cloth painted by women with dots, curves, and grids in charcoal, juice, and mud. Every panel is an individual composition — one of Africa's most abstract traditional visual idioms.
Dogon Satimbe Mask
Dogon · early–mid 20th c. · Mali
A tall wooden mask surmounted by a standing female figure with raised arms — the satimbe, "sister on the head." Worn by men to represent the mythic first woman who discovered the masks.
Ndebele boy in South Africa
by Cash, J. Allan
South Africa · ca. 1930-1970
Ndebele House Painting
Ndebele · 20th century · South Africa
Exterior walls painted freehand by women in sharp-edged chevrons, diamonds, and vivid blocks of color, applied without grid or ruler. The practice emerged after the 1883 Ndebele defeat as a coded language of resistance preserved inside everyday domestic space.
Bwa Plank Mask
Bwa · 20th c. · Burkina Faso
Towering rectangular mask crowned by a crescent, carved and painted in the Bwa Bayiri style. Worn at agricultural rites; the concentric patterns are protective scarification translated into wood.
Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
Yoruba · 20th-c. revival of traditional · Nigeria
A forest along the Oshun River filled with sculptures revived by the Austrian-Yoruba artist Susanne Wenger and local carvers — the last surviving intact sacred grove of the Yoruba.
Great Mosque of Touba
Mouride · 1963 · Senegal
Founded by the Sufi saint Amadou Bamba; the current mosque completed in the 1960s. Five minarets and a green dome; a gathering of millions every year at the Magal pilgrimage.
Sidibé Studio Portrait
by Malick Sidibé
Modern photography · 1960s–70s · Mali
Malick Sidibé shot Bamako's young for two decades — dancers at night parties, couples posed in matching bell-bottoms. A lens on independence-era West African youth at its most self-invented.
Shona Stone Sculpture
Shona · 20th c. onward · Zimbabwe
A post-colonial sculptural tradition carved from serpentine and springstone. Figures of spirits and ancestors, often semi-abstracted — now Zimbabwe's most recognized art export.
Shaka Zulu
by di Palma, Valentina
South Africa · 1986
Red Bird
by Chris Ofili
Contemporary · c. 2000s · United Kingdom / Nigeria
Chris Ofili's paintings layer resin-coated elephant dung onto glittering collage. His work interrogates Black identity and the politics of representation in Britain.
Earth's Skin
by El Anatsui
Contemporary · 2007 · El Anatsui
A shimmering 'cloth' made of thousands of liquor-bottle caps wired together — draped like a kente cloth but on an architectural scale. Hangs differently every time it's installed.
←Americas Egypt & Nubia→
Images via Wikimedia Commons. Click any image to open it and see its source.
